annotations.label: Non-Euclidean geometry

anchor.snippet: non-Euclidean geometry consists of two geometries based on axioms closely related to those that specify Euclidean geometry

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the hyperbolic plane (upper half-plane metric) as one non-Euclidean model, but has no general axiomatic framework for non-Euclidean geometry.

annotations.label: Playfair's postulate

anchor.snippet: which states that, within a two-dimensional plane, for any given line l and a point A , which is not on l , there is exactly one line through A that does not intersect l

annotations.note: No axiomatic synthetic-geometry formalization of Playfair's/parallel postulate exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parallels in hyperbolic vs elliptic geometry

anchor.snippet: there are infinitely many lines through A not intersecting l , while in elliptic geometry, any line through A intersects l

annotations.note: Mathlib has no synthetic notion of lines/parallels in hyperbolic or elliptic geometry to state this.

annotations.label: Ultraparallels

anchor.snippet: these lines are often called ultraparallels

annotations.note: No notion of ultraparallel lines in hyperbolic geometry is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclid's fifth (parallel) postulate

anchor.snippet: If a straight line falls on two straight lines in such a manner that the interior angles on the same side are together less than two right angles

annotations.note: Mathlib's Euclidean geometry is built on inner-product spaces, not Euclid's axioms, so the fifth postulate is not stated.

annotations.label: Euclid's first four postulates

anchor.snippet: To draw a straight line from any point to any point

annotations.note: Euclid's construction postulates are not formalized as axioms in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Khayyam's postulate

anchor.snippet: Two convergent straight lines intersect and it is impossible for two convergent straight lines to diverge in the direction in which they converge

annotations.note: No formalization of Khayyam's parallel-postulate substitute exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vitale's equidistance result

anchor.snippet: if three points are equidistant on the base AB and the summit CD, then AB and CD are everywhere equidistant

annotations.note: This equidistance result about Saccheri-type quadrilaterals is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lambert's angle-sum and area result

anchor.snippet: the sum of the angles in a triangle increases as the area of the triangle decreases

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the relation between triangle angle-sum (defect) and area in non-Euclidean geometry.

annotations.label: Absolute geometry

anchor.snippet: leaving all the other axioms intact, produces absolute geometry

annotations.note: Absolute (neutral) geometry as an axiomatic system is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Birkhoff's axiom

anchor.snippet: There exists a pair of similar but not congruent triangles

annotations.note: Birkhoff's similarity axiom is not formalized; Mathlib has similarity/congruence of triangles but not this axiom.

annotations.label: First 28 propositions hold in absolute geometry

anchor.snippet: the first 28 propositions of Euclid (in The Elements ) do not require the use of the parallel postulate or anything equivalent to it

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of Euclid's Elements propositions within absolute geometry.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic parallel axiom

anchor.snippet: there exist two lines through P, which do not meet l

annotations.note: The hyperbolic parallel axiom is not formalized as an axiom in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elliptic parallel axiom

anchor.snippet: all the lines through P meet l

annotations.note: The elliptic parallel axiom is not formalized as an axiom in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Models of non-Euclidean geometry

anchor.snippet: Models of non-Euclidean geometry are mathematical models of geometries that are non-Euclidean

annotations.note: Only the upper half-plane model of hyperbolic geometry is formalized; there is no general theory of non-Euclidean models.

annotations.label: Euclidean model (flat plane)

anchor.snippet: Euclidean geometry is modelled by our notion of a

annotations.note: The Euclidean plane is formalized as `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)`, though not framed as a zero-curvature model.

annotations.label: Pseudosphere model of hyperbolic geometry

anchor.snippet: The pseudosphere has the appropriate curvature to model hyperbolic geometry

annotations.note: The pseudosphere and its hyperbolic curvature are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sphere model of elliptic geometry

anchor.snippet: points opposite each other (called antipodal points ) are identified

annotations.note: Mathlib has an antipodal map on spheres but no construction of elliptic geometry by identifying antipodal points.

annotations.label: Sphere as real projective plane model

anchor.snippet: one of the standard models of the real projective plane

annotations.note: The real projective plane exists as `Projectivization ℝ (Fin 3 → ℝ)`, but its sphere-with-antipodal-identification model is not formalized.

annotations.label: Elliptic model: lines always intersect

anchor.snippet: all lines through A will intersect l

annotations.note: Mathlib has no elliptic-geometry incidence structure to express that all lines intersect.

annotations.label: Klein model

anchor.snippet: defined the Klein model , which models the entirety of hyperbolic space

annotations.note: Only the upper half-plane (Poincaré) model is in Mathlib; the Klein/Beltrami–Klein model is not formalized.

annotations.label: Equiconsistency of hyperbolic and Euclidean geometry

anchor.snippet: Euclidean geometry and hyperbolic geometry were equiconsistent so that hyperbolic geometry was logically consistent if and only if Euclidean geometry was

annotations.note: No metatheoretic equiconsistency result between hyperbolic and Euclidean geometry is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic model: infinitely many parallels

anchor.snippet: there are infinitely many lines through A that do not intersect l

annotations.note: The hyperbolic plane is formalized as a metric space, but the proposition about infinitely many non-intersecting lines is not stated.

annotations.label: Eight three-dimensional geometries

anchor.snippet: In three dimensions, there are eight models of geometries

annotations.note: Thurston's eight 3-dimensional geometries (geometrization) are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lambert quadrilateral

anchor.snippet: A Lambert quadrilateral is a quadrilateral with three right angles

annotations.note: The Lambert quadrilateral is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fourth angle of a Lambert quadrilateral

anchor.snippet: The fourth angle of a Lambert quadrilateral is acute if the geometry is hyperbolic, a right angle if the geometry is Euclidean or obtuse if the geometry is elliptic

annotations.note: No Lambert quadrilateral nor its fourth-angle classification is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rectangles exist only in Euclidean geometry

anchor.snippet: rectangles exist (a statement equivalent to the parallel postulate) only in Euclidean geometry

annotations.note: The equivalence of rectangle existence with the parallel postulate is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Saccheri quadrilateral

anchor.snippet: A Saccheri quadrilateral is a quadrilateral with two sides of equal length, both perpendicular to a side called the base

annotations.note: The Saccheri quadrilateral is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Summit angles of a Saccheri quadrilateral

anchor.snippet: The other two angles of a Saccheri quadrilateral are called the summit angles and they have equal measure

annotations.note: No Saccheri quadrilateral or summit-angle notion exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Summit angle type by geometry

anchor.snippet: The summit angles of a Saccheri quadrilateral are acute if the geometry is hyperbolic, right angles if the geometry is Euclidean and obtuse angles if the geometry is elliptic

annotations.note: The classification of Saccheri summit angles by geometry is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Triangle angle sum by geometry

anchor.snippet: The sum of the measures of the angles of any triangle is less than 180° if the geometry is hyperbolic, equal to 180° if the geometry is Euclidean, and greater than 180° if the geometry is elliptic

annotations.note: The Euclidean case (angles sum to π) is formalized, but the hyperbolic/elliptic inequalities are not.

annotations.label: Defect of a triangle

anchor.snippet: The defect of a triangle is the numerical value

annotations.note: The angular defect of a triangle is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sign of triangle defect by geometry

anchor.snippet: the defect of triangles in hyperbolic geometry is positive, the defect of triangles in Euclidean geometry is zero, and the defect of triangles in elliptic geometry is negative

annotations.note: The sign of the triangle defect by geometry type is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalized complex numbers

anchor.snippet: The points are sometimes identified with generalized complex numbers

annotations.note: The parametrized family of generalized complex numbers x²=p is not formalized, though special cases (Complex, DualNumber) exist separately.

annotations.label: Euclidean plane case

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean plane corresponds to the case

annotations.note: The ordinary complex numbers `Complex` (the Euclidean-plane case) are formalized, but not framed as a generalized-complex-number specialization.

annotations.label: Unit circle

anchor.snippet: is the unit circle

annotations.note: `Circle` is defined as the unit sphere of radius 1 centered at 0 in ℂ, i.e. the unit circle.

annotations.label: Split-complex number

anchor.snippet: Then z is a split-complex number and conventionally j replaces epsilon

annotations.note: Split-complex numbers (j²=1) are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unit hyperbola

anchor.snippet: is the unit hyperbola

annotations.note: The unit hyperbola is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dual number

anchor.snippet: then z is a dual number

annotations.note: `DualNumber R` formalizes numbers a+bε with ε²=0, exactly the dual numbers.

annotations.label: Non-Euclidean angles via polar decomposition

anchor.snippet: the parameters of slope in the dual number plane and hyperbolic angle in the split-complex plane correspond to angle in Euclidean geometry

annotations.note: The polar decomposition giving slope/hyperbolic-angle parameters in these planar algebras is not formalized.

annotations.label: Proper-time future submanifold is hyperbolic 3-space

anchor.snippet: could be considered a hyperbolic space of three dimensions

annotations.note: The identification of the proper-time future hypersurface with hyperbolic 3-space is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hyperboloid model

anchor.snippet: The relevant structure is now called the hyperboloid model of hyperbolic geometry

annotations.note: The hyperboloid (Minkowski) model of hyperbolic geometry is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Split-complex spacetime event

anchor.snippet: can represent a spacetime event one moment into the future of a frame of reference of rapidity a

annotations.note: Representing spacetime events via split-complex numbers and rapidity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multiplication as Lorentz boost

anchor.snippet: multiplication by z amounts to a Lorentz boost mapping the frame with rapidity zero to that with rapidity a

annotations.note: The interpretation of split-complex multiplication as a Lorentz boost is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dual numbers and shear mapping

anchor.snippet: Kinematic study makes use of the dual numbers

annotations.note: Dual numbers are formalized, but their kinematic use as Galilean shear mappings is not.
